topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara stared at the blueprints spread across the worn oak table. Her breath hitched, a small, sharp intake of air that seemed to steal the oxygen from the room. Beside her, Marcus was tracing a line on an identical schematic, his brow furrowed in concentration. The lines matched perfectly—the precise angles, the notation for the core mechanism, even the slightly smudged ink from a previous revision. A slow, creeping paralysis washed over Elara. She lifted a trembling hand, pointing a rigid finger at the document. "Marcus," she managed, her voice thin and reedy, "what is this?" Marcus looked up, his eyes meeting hers, and for a fleeting second, a strange, hollow look crossed his face before he quickly masked it with professional neutrality.

[story 2]

I dropped the soldering iron. It clattered onto the metal workbench with a loud, jarring sound that echoed in the cavernous workshop. My vision swam for a moment, the fluorescent lights above flickering like a dying star. Across the room, Liam was meticulously assembling the housing for the prototype. The wiring harness, the casing design, the very placement of the ventilation ports—it was an exact mirror image of the project I had been agonizing over for the last three weeks. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. I pushed myself up from the stool, my movements jerky and uncoordinated, and walked slowly toward him, my gaze fixed on the flawless replication of my own efforts.

[story 3]

The silence in the laboratory was absolute, thick enough to choke on. Clara leaned back in her chair, her fingers gripping the edge of the console until her knuckles turned white. Her gaze drifted from the complex data readouts to the physical model sitting on the central pedestal. It was flawless. Every component, every etched circuit board, every subtle curve of the casing—it was precisely the same as the one she had finished just hours ago. A low, involuntary hum vibrated in her chest, a feeling of profound disorientation. She slowly lowered her hand, letting it fall to her lap, and began to rub her temples, trying to physically scrub the impossible sight from her mind.
